According to O'Grady and Cho (2011), at the early stages of language acquisition, children ask yes–no questions by rising intonation alone as they develop awareness to auxiliary verbs only at a later stage. When auxiliary verbs make their appearance, it takes children a few months before they are able to use inversion in yes–no questions. The development of WH- questions occurs between the ages of two and four, when children acquire auxiliary verbs that then leads to the ability to use inversion in questions. However, some children find inversion easier in yes–no questions than in WH- questions, since the position of both the WH- word and the auxiliary verb must change (e.g., "You are going where?" instead of "Where are you going?").

=== Morphological development ===
Morphological structures development occurs over a period of several years. Before language is acquired, children lack any use of morphological structures.

The morphological structures that children acquire during their childhood, and even up to the early school years, are: determiners (a, the), -ing inflection, plural –s, auxiliary be, possessive –s, third person singular –s, past tense –ed). When children start using these structures, they tend to overgeneralize the rules and project them upon all the words in the language, including irregulars. For example, if a child knows the –ed (past tense) there is a possibility that they will say "I eated" (man-mans cat-cats). These errors result from overgeneralization of rules. This overgeneralization is very noticeable: in fact, children do that in less than 25 percent of the time at any point before reducing that percentage. Then they improve their mastery, which can be tested in various ways, such as the "wug test" (Berko, 1958).

Children often figure out quickly frequent irregular verbs, such as go and buy rather than less common ones, such as win. This suggests that children must hear the word several hundred times before they are able to use it correctly.

This development of bound morphemes is similar in order among children, for example: -''ing'' is acquired before the article ''the''. However, parents tend to use a different order while speaking to their kids, for example, parents use the article 'the' more frequently than -ing. Meaning, other factors determine the order of acquisition, such as:

# Morphemes that come at the end of an utterance are remembered better.
# Children seem to remember morphemes such as –ing better because they contain their own syllables rather than single consonant morpheme, such as plural –s.
# Affixes with more than one meaning are more difficult to acquire, e.g., –s functions both as plural noun and singular third.

As it comes to word formation processes such as derivation and compounding, the earliest derivational suffixes to show up in children's speech are those they hear adults use most frequently. (-er as the *'doer' of the action such as walker.) When it comes to compounds, children first make up names for agents and instruments that they don't know by a pattern (N-N), though some of them do not follow the pattern (*cutter grass for grass cutter). Then, they might have the right structure but the words are inappropriate since English already has words with the intended meaning such as car-smoke = exhaust. This process points to a preference for building words from other words, thus place less demand on memory than learning an entirely new word for each concept.

{{citation needed|date=December 2013}}

===Grammatical development===
Grammar describes the way sentences of a language are constructed, which includes both [[syntax]] and [[Morphology (linguistics)|morphology]].<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://dictionary.reference.com/browse/grammar|title = Grammar|website = Dictionary.com}}</ref>

====Syntactic development and morphological development====

Syntactic development involves the ways that various morphemes are ordered or combined in sentences.<ref>{{Cite web|title = the definition of syntactic|url = http://dictionary.reference.com/browse/syntactic|website = Dictionary.com|access-date = 2016-01-29}}</ref> [[Morpheme]]s, which are basic units of meaning in language, get added as children learn to produce simple sentences and become more precise speakers. Morphemes can be whole words (like "happy") or parts of words that change meaning of words ("<u>un"</u>happy). Brown<ref name="Brown" /> proposed a stage model that describes the various types of [[Morphology (linguistics)|morphological]] structures that are developed in English and the age range within which they are normally acquired.<ref name="Bowen_1998">{{Cite web|url = http://www.speech-language-therapy.com/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=33:brown&catid=2:uncategorised&Itemid=117|title = Brown's Stages of Syntactic Development|date = 1998|website = speech language therapy dot com| vauthors = Bowen C }}</ref>

'''Stage I:''' From '''15–30 months''', children start using telegraphic speech, which are two-word combinations, for example 'wet diaper'. Brown (1973)<ref name="Brown">{{cite book | vauthors = Brown R | date = 1973 | title = A first language: The early stages. | publisher = Harvard University Press }}</ref> observed that 75% of children's two-word utterances could be summarized in the existence of 11 [[semantic]] relations:<ref name="Brown" /><ref name="Bowen_1998" />{{div col|colwidth=22em}}
*Attributive: 'Big house'
*Agent-action: 'Daddy hit'
*Action-object: 'Hit ball'
*Agent-object: 'Daddy ball'
*Nominative: 'That ball'
*Demonstrative: 'There ball'
*Recurrence: 'More ball'
*Non-existence: 'All-gone ball'
*Possessive: 'Daddy chair'
*Entity + Locative: 'book table'
*Action + Locative: 'go store'
{{div col end}}

'''Stage II:''' At around '''28–36 months''', children begin to engage in the production of simple sentences, usually 3 word sentences. These simple sentences follow syntactic rules and are refined gradually as development<ref name="Bowen_1998" /> continues. The morphological developments seen in this age range include use of present progressive (<u>-ing</u> endings), the prepositions "in" and "on", and regular plurals (<u>-s</u> endings).<ref name="Bowen_1998" />

'''Stage III:''' Around '''36–42 months''', children continue to add morphemes and gradually produce complex grammatical structures.<ref name="Bowen_1998" /> The morphemes that are added at this age include irregular past tense, possessive ('s), and use of the verb 'to be' (It <u>is</u>, I <u>am</u>, etc.).<ref name="Bowen_1998" />

'''Stage IV:'''Around '''40–46 months''' children continue to add to their morphological knowledge. This range is associated with use of articles (<u>a</u> or <u>the</u>), regular past tense (<u>-ed</u> endings), and regular third person speech (<u>He likes</u> it).<ref name="Bowen_1998" />

'''Stage V:''' Around '''42-52+ months''' children refine the complex grammatical structures and increase their use of morphemes to convey more complex ideas. Children in this stage use irregular third-person speech, the verb 'to be' as an auxiliary verb (She <u>was not</u> laughing), and in its contraction forms (It<u>'s</u>, She<u>'s</u>, etc.).<ref name="Bowen_1998" />

=== Pragmatic development===
From birth to one year, babies can engage in [[joint attention]] (sharing the attention of something with someone else). Infants also can engage in turn taking activities<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Rochat P, Querido JG, Striano T | title = Emerging sensitivity to the timing and structure of protoconversation in early infancy | journal = Developmental Psychology | volume = 35 | issue = 4 | pages = 950–957 | date = July 1999 | pmid = 10442864 | doi = 10.1037/0012-1649.35.4.950 }}</ref> on the basis of their sensitivity to social contingency<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Tauzin |first=Tibor |date=2026 |title=Infants’ sensitivity to the predictability of exchanged actions in socially contingent exchanges: the contingency cube |url=https://royalsocietypublishing.org/rstb/article/381/1943/20240363/480042/Infants-sensitivity-to-the-predictability-of |journal=Philosophical Transactions B |language=en |volume=381 |issue=1943 |doi=10.1098/rstb.2024.0363 |issn=0962-8436}}</ref>, which can elicit social responses in the babies from very early on.<ref>{{Cite journal| vauthors = Watson JS |date=1972|jstor=23084026|journal=Merrill-Palmer Quarterly of Behavior and Development|volume=18|issue=4|pages=323–339|title=Smiling, Cooing, and "the Game"}}</ref> For example, most acquire early sensitivity to the [[prosody (linguistics)|prosodic patterns]] of social rituals such as peek-a-boo.<ref>{{citation |vauthors = Fernald, Anne, O'Neill DK|chapter= Peekaboo across cultures: How mothers and infants play with voices, faces, and expectations|editor= K. MacDonald| title=Parent–child play: Descriptions and implications |date=1993|publisher = State University of New York Press|pages=249–285}}</ref>

*By '''age 0.5–1 years''', infants understand that speech sounds can express intentions (such as requests) as opposed to coughs.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Vouloumanos |first=Athena |last2=Onishi |first2=Kristine H. |last3=Pogue |first3=Amanda |date=2012 |title=Twelve-month-old infants recognize that speech can communicate unobservable intentions |url=https://www.pnas.org/doi/full/10.1073/pnas.1121057109 |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=109 |issue=32 |pages=12933–12937 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1121057109 |pmc=3420163 |pmid=22826217}}</ref> They also recognize that variability of exchanged signal sequences and [[turn-taking]] are prerequisites of communicative information transfer.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Tauzin |first=Tibor |last2=Gergely |first2=György |date=2019 |title=Variability of signal sequences in turn-taking exchanges induces agency attribution in 10.5-mo-olds |url=https://pnas.org/doi/full/10.1073/pnas.1816709116 |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |language=en |volume=116 |issue=31 |pages=15441–15446 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1816709116 |issn=0027-8424 |pmc=6681728 |pmid=31308230}}</ref>
*By '''age 1–2 years''', they can engage in turn-taking exchanges and understand that turn-taking interactions of observed others can convey goal-relevant, communicative information.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tauzin T, Gergely G | title = Communicative mind-reading in preverbal infants | language = En | journal = Scientific Reports | volume = 8 | issue = 1 | page = 9534 | date = June 2018 | pmid = 29934630 | pmc = 6015048 | doi = 10.1038/s41598-018-27804-4 | bibcode = 2018NatSR...8.9534T }}</ref>
*By '''age 3–5 years''', children can master illocutionary intent, knowing what you meant to say even though you might not have said it and turnabout, which is turning the conversation over to another person.{{citation needed|date=December 2013}}
*By '''age 6–10 years''', shading occurs, which is changing the conversation topic gradually. Children are able to communicate effectively in demanding settings, such as on the telephone.{{citation needed|date=December 2013}}

===Effect of bilingualism===

There is a large debate regarding whether or not [[Multilingualism|bilingualism]] is truly [https://bilingualkidspot.com/2017/05/23/benefits-of-being-bilingual/ beneficial] to children. Parents of children often view learning a second language throughout elementary and high school education beneficial to the child.<ref name="Bialystok 2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = Bialystok E, Craik FI, Luk G | title = Bilingualism: consequences for mind and brain | journal = Trends in Cognitive Sciences | volume = 16 | issue = 4 | pages = 240–250 | date = April 2012 | pmid = 22464592 | pmc = 3322418 | doi = 10.1016/j.tics.2012.03.001 }}</ref> Another perspective dictates that the second language just confuses the child and prevents them from mastering their primary language.<ref name="Blumenfeld 2009 10">{{cite journal| vauthors = Blumenfeld H, Faroqi-Shah Y |title=Bilingualism: Consequences for language, cognition, development, and the brain|journal= The ASHA Leader|year=2009|volume=14|issue=13|pages=10–13 |doi=10.1044/leader.FTR2.14132009.10 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Studies have shown that American bilingual children have greater [[cognitive flexibility]], better perceptual skills and tend to be more divergent thinkers than [[Monolingualism|monolingual]] children between the ages of five and ten.<ref name="Bialystok 2012"/><ref name="Blumenfeld 2009 10"/> Better executive functioning skills are likely because bilingual children have to choose one language to speak while actively suppressing the other. This builds stronger selective attention and cognitive flexibility because these skills are being exercised more.<ref name = "Poulin-Dubois 2011">{{cite journal | vauthors = Poulin-Dubois D, Blaye A, Coutya J, Bialystok E | title = The effects of bilingualism on toddlers' executive functioning | journal = Journal of Experimental Child Psychology | volume = 108 | issue = 3 | pages = 567–579 | date = March 2011 | pmid = 21122877 | pmc = 4346342 | doi = 10.1016/j.jecp.2010.10.009 }}</ref> In addition, bilingual children have a better understanding of universal language concepts, such as grammar, because these concepts are applied in multiple languages.<ref name = "Katsos 2012">{{Cite journal | vauthors = Katsos N, Ezeizabarrena MJ, Gavarro A, Kraljevic JK, Hrzica G, Grohmann KK, Skordi A, Lopez KJ, Sundahl L, Van Hout A, Hollebrandse B | display-authors = 6 | title = The acquisition of quantification across languages: some predictions. | journal = Cascadilla Press }}</ref> However, studies comparing Swedish-Finnish bilingual children and Swedish monolingual children between the ages of five and seven have also shown that the bilingual children have a smaller vocabulary than monolingual children.<ref name="Korkman 2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = Korkman M, Stenroos M, Mickos A, Westman M, Ekholm P, Byring R | title = Does simultaneous bilingualism aggravate children's specific language problems? | journal = Acta Paediatrica | volume = 101 | issue = 9 | pages = 946–952 | date = September 2012 | pmid = 22591054 | doi = 10.1111/j.1651-2227.2012.02733.x | s2cid = 23821236 | doi-access = free }}</ref> In another study throughout America, elementary school English-monolingual children performed better in mathematics and reading activities than their non-English-dominant bilingual and non-English monolingual peers from kindergarten to grade five.<ref name="Han 2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = Han WJ | title = Bilingualism and academic achievement | journal = Child Development | volume = 83 | issue = 1 | pages = 300–321 | year = 2012 | pmid = 22098584 | doi = 10.1111/j.1467-8624.2011.01686.x }}</ref> Learning two languages simultaneously can be beneficial or a hindrance to a child's language and intellectual development. Further research is necessary to continue to shed light on this debate.

In addition to the study of bilingualism in children, similar research is being conducted in adults. Research findings show that although [https://bilingualkidspot.com/2017/05/23/benefits-of-being-bilingual/ bilingual benefits] are muted in middle adulthood,<ref name="Bialystok 2012" /> they are more profound in older age when those who develop dementia experience onset about 4.5 years later in bilingual subjects.<ref name = "Alladi 2013">{{cite journal | vauthors = Alladi S, Bak TH, Duggirala V, Surampudi B, Shailaja M, Shukla AK, Chaudhuri JR, Kaul S | display-authors = 6 | title = Bilingualism delays age at onset of dementia, independent of education and immigration status | journal = Neurology | volume = 81 | issue = 22 | pages = 1938–1944 | date = November 2013 | pmid = 24198291 | doi = 10.1212/01.wnl.0000436620.33155.a4 | s2cid = 18874131 }}</ref> The increased attentional control, inhibition, and conflict resolution developed from bilingualism may be accountable for the later onset of dementia.<ref name="Bialystok 2012" />

==== Misdiagnoses as a language impairment ====
There is some research that demonstrates that bilingualism may often be misdiagnosed as a language impairment.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kohnert K | title = Bilingual children with primary language impairment: issues, evidence and implications for clinical actions | journal = Journal of Communication Disorders | volume = 43 | issue = 6 | pages = 456–473 | date = 2010 | pmid = 20371080 | pmc = 2900386 | doi = 10.1016/j.jcomdis.2010.02.002 }}</ref> A subtopic of bilingualism in the literature is nonstandard varieties of English.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Stockman IJ | title = A review of developmental and applied language research on African American children: from a deficit to difference perspective on dialect differences | journal = Language, Speech, and Hearing Services in Schools | volume = 41 | issue = 1 | pages = 23–38 | date = January 2010 | pmid = 20051577 | doi = 10.1044/0161-1461(2009/08-0086) | s2cid = 12626506 }}</ref> While bilingualism and nonstandard varieties of English cannot be considered a true language impairment, they are misrepresented in the population of those receiving language interventions.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Dockrell JE, Marshall CR | title = Measurement Issues: Assessing language skills in young children | journal = Child and Adolescent Mental Health | volume = 20 | issue = 2 | pages = 116–125 | date = May 2015 | pmid = 32680388 | doi = 10.1111/camh.12072 | url = https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/1492756/ }}</ref>

== Language disorders ==
A [[language disorder]] is the impaired comprehension and or use of a spoken, written, or other symbol system. A disorder may involve problems in the following areas: 
#The form of language i.e. [[phonology]], [[Morphology (linguistics)|morphology]], or [[syntax]] {{citation needed|date=December 2013}}
#The content i.e. semantics{{citation needed|date=December 2013}}
#The function of language in communication i.e. pragmatics {{citation needed|date=December 2013}}

Olswang and colleagues have identified a series of behaviors in children in the 18–36-month range that are predictors for the need of language intervention.<ref name="Olswang 1998">{{Cite web  | vauthors = Olswang LB, Rodriguez B, Timler G | title = Recommending Intervention for Toddlers With Specific Language Learning Difficulties | url =http://courses.washington.edu/sop/RecInterventionToddlers.pdf | publisher = American Journal of Speech-Language Pathology| volume= 7 | pages= 23–32 | date = 1998| access-date = 9 December 2013 }}</ref>

These predictors include: 
* A smaller than average vocabulary 
* A language comprehension delay of 6 months or a comprehension deficit with a large comprehension production gap 
* Phonological problems such as restricted [[babbling]] or limited vocalizations 
* Few spontaneous vocal imitations and reliance on direct modeling in imitation tasks 
* Little combinatorial or symbolic play 
* Few communicative or symbolic gestures 
* Behavior problems<ref name="Olswang 1998"/>

Some of the many conditions that cause language development problems include:
{{div col|colwidth=22em}}
*[[Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder]]
*[[Auditory processing disorder]]
*[[Autism]]
*[[Developmental verbal dyspraxia]]
*[[Language delay]]
*[[Otitis media]]
*[[Pragmatic language impairment]]
*[[Specific language impairment]]
*[[Speech delay]]
*[[Speech disorder]]
*[[Speech sound disorder]]
*[[Speech repetition]]
{{div col end}}
